posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked: A well-known Burgundy critic recently deemed a bottle I was thinking of opening “very Narvaux.” (It was a 2017 PYCM.) I couldn’t help but do a doubletake: really? I feel like on a good day, I can possibly distinguish from one another classic Meursault, Puligny, ans Chassagne, though probably not St. Aubin–but a given lieux dit from another? From Les Tillets right next to it, for example? I know I don’t have the experience or the extraordinarily sensitive palate of a seriously good critic and this critic is just that. But it did make me stop and wonder and wonder what others might think.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I do think it’s a little fanciful, especially since Narvaux is such a large climat and between the north and the south and the bottom and the top one should expect rather different styles of wine! I actually made a bit of Narvaux in 2022 so I have seen this first hand.

However it isn’t at all fanciful to distinguish between the profiles of hillside sites along the lines of Tillets, Narvaux, Clous, Vireuils etc and lower-lying sites such as Limouzin, Pelles, Cromin etc.

To me, there is more family resemblance between sites such as Chassagne-Montrachet Pot Bois, Puligny-Montrachet Le Trezin and Meursault Tillets, for example, than there is between those sites and their lower-lying cousins at the bottom of the slope in their three respective communes. If you talk with geologists you will rapidly realize that the communal boundaries seldom correspond with distinctive geologies. Indeed, tended to grow up on alluvial fans at the foot of ravines that in fact mark shifts in the geology of the Côte d’Or’s escarpment, so this is hardly surprising.

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked:
Thanks for this, William,
It’s very much appreciated! It’s cool that you got to make some of your own Narvaux. Other than PYCM, I think of Lafon when I think of this site. Are there other producers whose expressions you like–besides, of course, your own?

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
Well, I’ m no specialist on Village Meursault sites, although I know and own Narvaux.
Generally, a Grand Cru usually has a very strong personality, the better Premier Crus, too … if slightly less distinctive … and the differences between Village lieu-dits are often not really pronounced … yes, in a certain cellar it’s often clear … but with different producers

I have a certain picture in mind of a typical Richebourg, RSV … or also a VR Suchots versus a VR Reignots … but Aux Reas agains Hautes Maizieres ? I’d be happy if both are textbook Vosnes …
Laurels for anybody who can recognize a M.Narvaux blind …
posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

Jobard, Lafon, Boyer-Martenot, Michelot … and I had a case of 2004 Moret-Nominet that’s still drinking fine …

Joshua_Kates:

I don’t see many Michelot whites for sale here in the US; agree about some of the others–I haven’t tried them all, including Chavy Chouet. Moret-Nominet?

I misspelled it (sorry): Moret-Nominé - now probably only David MORET (Beaune/Savigny-les-Beaune) is a very small negociant, without any own vines, producing only whites in very small quantities - I bought several wines vintage 2004/05 at auction, incl. Montrachet GC, Meursault Gouttes d´Or and Narvaux … outstanding if initially a bit heavy in new oak, but that has softened now, reminded me a bit on Lafon … (but I only know these 2 vintages)

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I don’t really agree with regards to the lieux-dits of Meursault. Many of the hillside sites were not planted in 1943 when the premier cru classification was made, as they had been left fallow after phylloxera. Roulot’s Tesson wasn’t planted until 1959. They certainly would be strong contenders for an upgrade today if the classification were to be revised; and there really is a big stylistic difference between the wines produced by these hillside sites and those from the foot of the slope and the alluvial fans.

reply_to_user.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
The benchmark, unhelpfully, is d’Auvenay, which comes from two rather long parcels towards the southern end of the climat where the soils contain more fine limestone fragments (the result of freeze-thaw erosion). This is a wine of amazing density and structure, animated by a bright spine of acidity, and it is often one of the best wines in the range if one looks beyond the classification and order of tasting.

You can see a d’Auvenay parcel, among others, here: William Kelley on Instagram: "Harvesting in Meursault Les Narvaux, seen from the air! With the 2022 vintage well underway, it’s full of promise. Despite the year’s challenges, principally very dry conditions, first impressions of the fruit are promising, with the potential for good maturity, rather moderate alcohol levels, perfect sanitary conditions and good weather for harvesting. However, the extremes of the year have thrown agronomic choices into relief, and the broad latitude it offers in terms of harvest dates will certainly make generalizing about 2022 difficult when it’s time to taste them from barrel next year! Video courtesy of @wordonthegrapevine"

Coche-Dury owns a parcel in Narvaux, towards the bottom of the vineyard towards the northern end, and this is bottled as Meursault. When you taste them knowing which is which, it exhibits a bit more plenitude and flesh than Vireuils, but more tension and cut than Pelles/Lauraule. There’s also a hefty dollop of Narvaux in several Meursault blends, such as PYCM’s these days (he hasn’t been bottling Narvaux apart the last couple of years) and Henri Boillot’s.

For other examples, Ballot-Millot make a strong contender in a rather leesy, reductive style, the 2020 is especially good. It’s a domaine cuvée for Maison Vincent Girardin, and Eric Germain has been doing a terrific job in recent years - two winters on the lees, carefully chosen cooperage, I buy it for my own cellar and when I have been able to convince friends to do the same, they have been delighted. Jobard-Morey has been on a rising trajectory in recent years and the 2020 is the best to date; and the same can be said for Boyer-Martenot, already cited here.

posts.name: Troy Stark
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Ballot-Millot make a strong contender in a rather leesy, reductive style, the 2020 is especially good. It’s a domaine cuvée for Maison Vincent Girardin

So, to be clear, we’re looking for Maison Vincent Girardin Meursault “Les Narvaux” for the one made by Ballot-Millot? Thank you!

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked:
Thanks for all this, William,
Fascinating! Yes, it is unfortunate that d’Auvenay is the benchmark. But I will keep an eye out for some of the others that you and Gerhard recommended. After your comments on another thread, I have a different Girardin (2017 C-M Morgeot) I am intending to open, so I’ll see whether to pursue that one and I do own PYCM’s from that vintage, which I assume is worth the quaff. @Troy_Stark though I am obviously not William, I believe you are conflating 2 of his recommendations: Ballot-Millot Narvaux, which is its own production and label, and Girardin’s now made by his son Eric, which still travels under the Vincent Girardin name.

posts.name: Troy Stark
posts.cooked: Ah yes, I was confused by the wording. I thought “It’s” was referring to what had just came before (the reference to Ballot-Millot) and not to the overall topic, so I was utterly confused. Derp.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Almost correct - Girardin was sold to Boisset, and is run by the very able Eric Germain, who isn’t any relation to Vincent Girardin (Vincent’s son is PVG), but is rather the brother of JF Germain of Domaine Henri Germain.

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked: Got it! I know of Henri Germain and his wines. But Vincent G’s son is Pierre? Who now produces under his own label?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Vincent’s son is Pierre Vincent Girardin. The label is Pierre (Vincent) Girardin. I suppose to avoid issues they go by Pierre Girardin now, but the initials PVG are on the label very prominently and that’s what everyone in Burgundy calls it.

posts.name: Keith Levenberg
posts.cooked: The critic you’re talking about does this all the time - it belongs on @John_Morris’s bingo card.

posts.name: Dav1d S@wyer
posts.cooked: And speaking of, PVG makes an awesome Narvaux himself.

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked: Yes, that’s true and “definitely worth considering.”

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Just made a video that might also answer some of your questions: William Kelley on Instagram: "Back in the vineyards, this time with a walk around some of the hillside lieux-dits of Meursault, looking at the likes of Tesson, Meix Chavaux, Luchets, Vireuils, Clous, Tillets and Narvaux, and discussing what they have in common, how they differ, and how they relate to our idea of “Meursault”! Note that my internal compass was a bit off, and Tesson is already more East than Southeast facing, so don’t go failing a WSET exam after being mislead by me 😅"
